title: Core inflation causes headaches for RBA
full_text:
Economists have taken little comfort from weaker-than-expected headline inflation figures after the Reserve Bank’s preferred trimmed mean measure rose to a higher than expected 3.6 per cent in the year to May, creating headaches for the bank in its fight against inflation.
The trimmed, or core, inflation annual figure, closely watched by the RBA, jumped from 3.4 per cent to 3.6 per cent in May – leaving the measure at its highest level in almost two years.
Core inflation’s upside surprise in the Australian Bureau of Statistics May reading will keep pressure on the monetary policy board to continue hiking rates, after it decided to keep the cash rate steady at 4.35 per cent earlier this month.
KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne claimed another rate rise was now “guaranteed”.
“Unfortunately today’s data adds to the case for a rate hike. There’s two numbers we look at – the most critical being core inflation, and frustratingly we are seeing this rise which will give the RBA the justification it needs to increase rates, most likely in August,” Mr Rynne said.
“Beyond that, it’s difficult to predict what will happen but at least one more rise is guaranteed.” Unemployment figures released on Thursday will also be a key consideration for the RBA during its interest rate deliberations at the monetary policy board’s August meeting.
Jim Chalmers welcomed the drop in headline inflation from 4.2 per cent down to 4 per cent, touting a substantial drop in automotive fuel – artificially suppressed by the government’s halving of the fuel excise tax.
“We are realistic, we’re not complacent about the future trajectory of inflation, but you’d rather headline inflation coming down than going up,” the Treasurer said.
“Automotive fuel fell 11.9 per cent in the month, but was still 7.7 per cent higher in through the year terms. So that extension of the fuel excise relief, in a tapered way, provides cost of living help, it recognises that there’s still some uncertainty in the Middle East, and that the costs and consequences of that war will play out for some time.” Dr Chalmers also noted that inflation has been ticking up in comparative Western countries.
But Australia’s CPI remains higher than almost every other economy’s apart from the US, with Britain at 2.8 per cent, Canada at 3.2 per cent, Japan at 1.5 per cent, and Germany at 2.6 per cent.
Australia’s headline inflation has also risen steadily across nine months before the falls in the past two months.
Deloitte Access Economic Partner Stephen Smith confirmed the temporary handout had masked the extent of Australia’s inflation problem, forecasting at least one more rate rise from the Reserve Bank this year.
“The government’s temporary fuel excise cut has masked the extent to which inflation pressures remain a problem for the Australian economy and is delaying some of the price growth pass-through to other sectors. These pressures will become more visible as the policy is unwound through July,” he said.
The ABS release confirmed housing-related costs continued to be a primary driver of inflation, with electricity prices rising 21.1 per cent over the year to May – partly driven by the roll-off of state and federal electricity rebates.
The figures come as Australia’s energy regulator revealed the number of electricity customers entering hardship programs had reached a five-year high in the first quarter of the calendar year.
There were 67,860 electricity customers who entered hardship programs in the first three months of the year, the AER confirmed in its latest quarterly report.
WHAT CHALMERS SAID ON WEDNESDAY 'It’s also worth remembering that in the most recent numbers, we’ve seen inflation tick up in the US and in Canada, and in Europe, but it has come down again in the Australian economy'
'It’s also worth remembering that inflation is much lower, than when we came to office, it was north of 6 per cent and rapidly rising'
... AND WHAT HE DIDN’T SAY CPI had steadily risen across nine months before the falls in the past two months Australia’s CPI remains higher than almost every major economy’s, except the US
